data.title: D.R. Horton: Robust Operations And Liquidity Warrant An Upside
data.description:
The residential property market sees various challenges amid macroeconomic volatility and skyrocketing prices. Yet, D.R. Horton, Inc. (NYSE: DHI) remains stable and well-positioned with its strategic operations. Its robust liquidity position ensures the sustainability of its operations and dividends, making it an attractive portfolio addition. That is why I believe the stock is reasonably priced with some upside potential. This is supported by its improving technicals with early bullish signals.

Residential Property Market Remains Challenged

D.R. Horton is not completely insulated from market and macroeconomic headwinds. This is amplified by inflation and the Fed’s cautious stance. As of today, inflation remains stubborn at 2.7%, while the Fed does not signal a probability of policy easing continuation in the near future. With that, these continue to impact the purchasing power of many consumers.

Meanwhile, the housing market has its fair share of headwinds aside from these macroeconomic indicators. In the past two years, home sales have dropped dramatically amid the skyrocketing home prices and mortgage rates. As a result, vacancy rates across the US bounced back.

Currently, the median home sales price in the US is $416.900. In the chart below, we can see that the price remains elevated. In fact, it has stayed above $400,000 for 12 consecutive quarters. There has been only gradual weakness since Q4 2022 at $442,600. I think this is the impact of the decreasing home demand in the past three years. However, it is still not enough to push the price back to pre-pandemic levels.

In the same way, DHI’s homes closed and average prices decreased. The highest price drop was in Southeast at -6.6%. Southeast also had the highest decrease in homes closed by 987 units. Despite this, Northwest and North remained resilient as their prices increased by 8.9% and 1.6%, respectively. They partially offset the weakness in other regions. Overall, the average home prices of DHI only changed by -3.3% YoY. In addition, lowering home prices worked in South Central and East, as homes closed rose by 135 and 87 units, respectively.

Now, Will There Be A Bubble Burst?

I understand the woes of many investors and analysts. The US has gone through a series of crises like the Great Recession and the Real Estate Crash. We may be wondering if it will happen again anytime soon.

But in my view, I don’t think there will be another real estate crash or GFC version 2.0 for several reasons. First, homebuilders have been very cautious about their home inventories since the GFC. Over the past decade, home inventories have generally decreased. We can attribute it to their slow construction as they remained careful amid the US economic recovery. Construction phases were further slowed down by lockdowns in 2020. Although it increased during the 2021-2022 housing boom, it never went back to pre-GFC levels.

Moreover, homebuilders were quick to react to the waning home demand amid skyrocketing prices since 2022. We can see that construction starts and homes under construction have also dropped in the past three years. These trends offset the trend of housing demand in the US. So, they balance home supply and demand today.

Considering this, it will take a lot of time for prices to decrease or return to pre-pandemic levels. Again, I don’t think there will be a crash. But it’s possible for the price to gradually decrease and normalize at $350,000-$400,000 after two or three years if we look at how homebuyers and homebuilders react. By that time, I expect the US economy to be more stable- inflation normalized at 2.0% and interest rates ranging at 2.50%-3.50%, so property builders and consumers will also cope with these trends. DHI remains in line with it.

Why D.R. Horton Will Remain Robust

Amid all the challenges today, DHI remains resilient with its robust operations. One thing I like about it is its well-diversified business. It covers six regions in the US, allowing it to maintain a huge domestic market presence.

It also allows it to diversify prices based on the market conditions per region. This gives it an advantage in pricing flexibility. As we can see, the average price of homes for net sales orders is only $365,060, lower than the US median home prices. This shows that DHI affords to lower its prices without sacrificing its profitability. As a result, the number of homes recorded in its net sales orders rose from 23,001 to 23,071. This is a slight rebound from the weaker performance during Q1 and Q2 2025. With this new demand, DHI secures new revenues.

This is supported by its prudent geographical diversification. It has a high concentration in South Central, covering states like Oklahoma and Texas. In my opinion, doing this is a prudent move since South Central appears to be its best-performing market in terms of demand. We can see that the number of homes closed and net sales orders increased YoY. Aside from that, Texas is one of the largest economies in the US with the huge number of businesses and robust oil and gas activities there. Texas is also home to many high-net-worth individuals or HNWIs in the US. Houston and Dallas ranked fifth and sixth in among the US cities with the most HNWIs at 90,900 and 68,600, respectively. So, it’s not surprising that home demand is also resilient in the state.

Another thing I like about DHI is its prudent inventory management. Like most homebuilders, DHI responds very well to the current market environment. DHI also works on its inventory management to avoid unnecessary operating costs. It continues to reduce its homes in inventory, especially unsold homes, to also lower its spending amid the weaker demand and pricing power. This helps it stabilize costs and margins. It is not surprising that it maintains a double-digit net income margin of 11.1% last Q3.

Lastly, DHI maintains a strong liquidity position with its robust cash flow. As we can see, its operating cash flow has always been high enough to cover capex and acquisition costs. Its FCF of $2.8M means it converts 8% of its sales into free cash using the FCF/Sales Ratio. It complements this with prudent debt management to cover repayment on time and sustain dividend payouts and share repurchases. It also has a reasonable debt maturity profile as 33% of the total amount has a maturity of at least five years. But even if maturity is only one year, DHI can still cover it because its Net Debt/EBITDA is only 0.9x. This means that DHI can cover all repayments at once in less than one year even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, DHI maintains a high capital buffer and returns despite being a capital-intensive business.

How I Think It Will Perform

I estimated its 2025 performance using two methods. First, I used its CAGR of 3.2% to derive its revenue. Note that I started in 2022 so as not to include the impact of the pandemic and the housing boom in 2021. Also, focusing on the past three years is wise since the current market trend began in that year. So, the operating revenue will be $38.0B. For COGS and operating expenses, I set them as a percentage of revenue at 76% and 8%, respectively. These are based on their historical performance. To be more precise, I also included depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. The ending NOPAT will be $4.7B.

However, I think this estimation is too optimistic considering its weaker YoY performance in recent quarters. With that in mind, I just based my estimation on its performance in the past three quarters. This will lead to an estimated revenue of $33.1B. This is lower than the company guidance of $33.7-34.2B. Yet, I believe it’s more conservative. For COGS and operating expenses, I also set them as a percentage of revenue. But I included a 2.7% increase to adjust inflation. So, the ending NOPAT will only be $3.6B. Despite this, it is important to note that DHI will stay profitable. In fact, NOPAT is 10.8% of the total revenue. That will be the returns it can generate from its core operations.

Valuation

DHI has increased over the years, and I believe that’s justifiable considering its BVPS and FCF per share or FCFs. The price was most consistent with these two metrics, so I decided to focus on them. Currently, P/B shows that it trades at 1.84x its BVPS. This is lower than the five-year average of 2.07x, so it is still relatively cheap. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $166.46 or a 12% upside potential. The same applies to P/FCF as DHI trades lower than its historical average. This suggests a much higher TP of $218.78.

To have a more precise valuation, I also used the DCF Model. I just used my estimation in the previous section. To be more conservative, I used the second method, where revenue will be below the company guidance. For 2026-2029, I just set their revenue growth at 3.0%. This is quite lower than its three-year CAGR of 3.2% just to be conservative. For its perpetual growth rate, I simply set it at 2.0% to be consistent with inflation. Meanwhile, I estimated WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. I also set its risk premium at 5.0% to compensate for the risk in this stock. This derives a TP of $160.44, which also shows underpricing. If I add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will be $152.42. Hence, DHI is still reasonably cheap at this price.

Technicals

DHI has early bullish signals as it rebounds from its recent correction. This is supported by its bullish fair value gap after its Q3 release. On top of that, the price has already exceeded the 200-day SMA, showing improved momentum. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA has shifted its direction upward, showing optimistic market sentiments recently. A bullish confirmation will take place if it also crosses above the 200-day SMA.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI still mirror the price trend. So, there is no bearish divergence yet. My only concern is that entering can be challenging as the stock is still nearly overbought. The good thing is that momentum remains strong as the MACD line remains above the signal line. Also, the histogram is within positive territory. This shows that bulls are still in control.

We can also note that the price jumped to the 0.50 Fibonacci Retracement point after the Q3 release. Considering the overbought situation, it is possible that the price may retrace to 0.382 as buyers wait for a better entry point. Once more buyers enter, the price may reach the 0.618 point at $165.7, which is also in line with my P/B TP.

Conclusion

D.R. Horton, Inc. remains an attractive company with its prudent pricing strategy, inventory management, enhanced efficiency, and geographical diversification. This is supported by its robust liquidity, which ensures its sustainability amid a challenging environment. In addition, the stock stays reasonably priced with some upside potential. Technicals are also attractive as bullish signals continue. Hence, I am giving this stock a strong buy rating.

data.title: FTEC: The Right Core, But Not The Complete Picture
data.description:
The Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FTEC) tracks the MSCI USA IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index, much like VGT, and suffers from the same drawbacks. My first preference in today's market environment is a DIY portfolio, even in tech. More so because the valuations are stretched and not all ETFs are not ideally loaded, dragged by a backward looking market cap factor.

However, that standard advice aside, FTEC additionally suffers from lower AUM related issues, like bid ask spreads, compared to even VGT, which makes it even more difficult to Buy FTEC. The marginally lower fees at 0.08% (vs 0.09% for VGT) is minor nitpicking in the absence of any strong case for FTEC. Hence, FTEC is assigned a similar rating as VGT i.e. a Hold. This thesis will focus on how the setup is not favorable for tech focused portfolios like FTEC and VUG both, from some additional data and fundamental analysis I did.

Drawback Recap

The use of GICS classification means names like Amazon, Meta and Alphabet remain outside the portfolio. It is not a question of whether FTEC's current holdings are good enough. It is a question of inclusion for a thematic ETF in technology.

The other issue with the methodology is the backward looking market cap weighting that results in disproportionate concentration in the top 10. These levels of concentration is one of the highest in history. While concentration is still acceptable for a tech portfolio, FTEC takes it to another extreme. For a portfolio of close to 300 stocks, a top 3 contributing ~45%, reduces the presence of the last 100 names to almost nothing more than window dressing. And that is exactly why I am advocating DIY investing in tech today. The core portfolio diversification benefits in passive investing is looking lost, and instead investors are left with a backward looking rigid holding that reacts slowly.

Odds Against GICS Tech Portfolio

A check on total return of a GICS constrained portfolio against the a simple equal weighted magnificent 7 ETF like MAGS shows significant outperformance in the latter approach.

And the outperformance does not imply a deeper drawdown in stress scenarios either. In the recent tariff sell-off, MAGS was down as far as FTEC was, almost. I expect that to be the norm in theory given the diversification benefits of a highly disparate constituent behavior across the volatile tech stock collection. However, the outperformance is a surprise, although that is not a structural advantage. The gains in Meta have led that upside in the period of analysis. In another era, FTEC could gain if its bets on Nvidia and Microsoft outperforms.

To check performance over a larger tenure, I picked FNGS with a very highly concentrated bet on the broader tech mega caps including Amazon, Meta and Google. For a slightly worse drawdown profile (checked against the 2022 corrections), the returns differences are again impressively better.

Intra Tech Views

Lets pitch Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft against Amazon, Meta and Google and see how the former 3 (FTEC trio) are placed against the missing names (missing trio). The past performance is something that we have already seen where the latter 3's inclusion has driven up returns. Lets look at expectations ahead and factors that could predict future performance/risks.

The FTEC trio looks visibly the more quality, moat and capital discipline play in comparison to the missing trio. In my view, Microsoft remains the most convincing long-term compounder with end to end AI monetization potential and strong network effects. Apple appears growth constrained with the story more around capital return and income at this point. Nvidia tips the growth angle for the FTEC trio - comfortably the best growth story on paper across even the missing trio. However, it does carry elevated risks given valuations priced for perfection and ecosystem uncertainties.

On the other side, the missing trio seem to offer better growth overall (continuing the trend from the past). Risks are looking slightly higher than the FTEC trio, but not as significantly different as the upside is. Amazon is currently looking struck, but offers a long-term growth optionality through a multitude of diversified avenues. Retail margins are turning around a bit, ad monetization is kicking in and the AI infra play is promising. Meta seems to be the biggest optionality play - if execution matches the vision and potential then the upside is large here. But risks are elevated from spending and monetization unknowns. Google looks undervalued on Cloud and Waymo optionality, but also faces structural risks to margins and regulatory heat .

The Hold on FTEC

I feel the FTEC trio will be the core of my own DIY portfolio because its combines decent defense alongside growth levers from Nvidia. However, Amazon, Google and Meta will also earn their place for the optionality. The tactical rotation around the FTEC core could result in strong outperformance as seen historically. Unfortunately, FTEC does not have the luxury of tactical or even long-term diversification benefits.

FTEC is not a failing proposition. As mentioned, it gets the core right, even looking ahead from a tech portfolio perspective. But I am more comfortable with diversified tech if I have to do passive investing. Even QQQ is better placed for me now, given its more defensive allocation and lower concentration risks (although still quite high on an absolute basis).

FTEC is a Hold. And not only because tech valuations are looking discomforting. Or its holdings are not aligned to my viewpoint. I am more concerned overall about new age tech companies missing the bus here. That and the fact that if I have to go passive, I will like the comfort of more diversification. A seen in the past, a diversified tech approach works better in terms of overall risk reward profiles.

data.title: Credit Rating For The Unrated (Part 12): CTO Realty Growth
data.description:
We continue our series with CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (NYSE:CTO) and its outstanding preferred stock, CTO Realty Growth, Inc. 6.375% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (NYSE:CTO.PR.A).

Company Overview

CTO Realty Growth, Inc. is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates a portfolio of high-quality, retail-based properties located primarily in higher growth markets in the United States. CTO also externally manages and owns a meaningful interest in Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE), a publicly traded net lease REIT.

The company has $1.24 billion in total assets, and as of the writing of this article, the market cap is $570 million. CTO total return for the past 10 years is 38%, underperforming both VNQ and SPY.

CTO pays $0.38 dividend each quarter, which at current prices is about 8.78%.

The forward P/FFO is 9.38, and the forward funds from operations yield comes at 5.3%. Key valuation metrics are displayed below:

The company is focused on open-air shopping centres in the Southwest and Southeast. Please see below a slide from their Q1 2025 presentation:

The portfolio composition is also described in the presentation:

  • Asset coverage ratio of 206%
  • Earns 9.45% gross in rental
  • Operating expenses, excluding depreciation, are approximately 38.79% of the total revenue
  • Interest expenses are 3.82% of the debt
  • Asset yield is 10.61% and market adjusted is 10.98%

Preferred Stock

There is only one preferred - CTO Realty Growth, Inc. 6.375% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock. Below we have listed its key metrics.

The security has a current yield of 7.49% that is fixed and cumulative. It also has a change of control clause, which is the lesser of Par Value (25) + accrued dividends or a conversion of 2.8218 per share. It must be noted that the conversion clause in the prospectus is 0.9406 per share, but since there was a stock split 3:1 in 2022, it adjusts to 2.8218 per share.

Credit Rating

We follow Moody's methodology with some adjustments to evaluate the creditworthiness of the company.

1. Gross assets: The total assets of 1.3 billion fall into Ba3 category

2. Unencumbered assets: Percentage of 49% is defined as Ba2

3. Secured debt: With 32% of secured debt to gross assets we assign a B2 rating

4. Total debt + preferred stock : 54% is leading us to Ba2 rating

5. Net debt to EBITDA: Using total debt and EBITDA of $79 million we are coming to a rating of Caa2

6. Fixed charge coverage. EBITDA/(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock), the company scores Ba3

The balance sheet score and the adjusted overall score are both Ba3. In the adjusted balance sheet score, we have taken into consideration:

  • Market Positioning - B1
  • Operating Environment - B1
  • Access to Capital - Baa3

CTO has focused its open-air retail portfolio in places with fast growth, positive job migration, and concentrated population:

As usual, we score the preferred stock two notches lower to B2, which for current yield of 7.49%, when you have A1 preferreds trading at 6.50% is a bit overpriced.

Before we conclude, we must address the elephant in the room, which is the short report from Wolfpack. We do not own CTO or CTO.PR.A, as neither of them fit our criteria for yield to risk ratio. This being said, if the allegations are true, they would have an impact on our credit score. However, we are using official data regulated by the SEC, and as investors, we rely on the regulatory body to do its job in such cases. We hope that management will address any doubts at the upcoming earnings on the 29th of July 2025, and we will write an update if needed. Our recommendation will be for the preferred stock investors to switch to a different instrument, not because of the short report, but the 7.49% current yield for a B2 rating is not adequate in today's market environment. You can buy investment-grade instruments with the same yield.

Conclusion

CTO Realty Growth scores Ba3 rating and the preferred B2. We find the yield of the preferred underwhelming at current prices and recommend switching to a different security.

data.title: Alphabet: Who Said Search Can't Still Be Sexy?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Alphabet Inc. aka Google (GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL), in April 2025, I analyzed the company’s Q1 earnings report and examined how the management was navigating the macroeconomic challenges and what impact the challenges at the time had on the company’s capex spending. I also examined the progress made by both Google Cloud and Waymo. I had a Buy rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, the stock has gained 19.5%, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained 15.4% during the same period.

In this article, I analyze the company’s Q2 earnings report and examine the key takeaways from the quarter and their implications on investors. I also deploy a predictive model using machine learning to determine GOOGL’s valuation.

A Snapshot of Second Quarter Results

Alphabet had a good quarter in my opinion, beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates once again. More specifically, Q2 revenues came in at $96.43 billion, up 14% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $2.45 billion. Diluted EPS came in at $2.31, up 22% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.12. Operating margins came in strong at 32.4% but were flat y/y on account of higher legal costs, with a $1.4 billion charge related to a settlement being the primary driver of operating expenses. Free cash flows for the quarter, on account of higher capital expenditures, dipped to $5.3 billion, with trailing twelve-month cash flows coming in at $66.7 billion.

While management didn’t provide any specific outlook, they did provide commentary on what to expect for the rest of the year and for 2026. The key takeaway from the commentary is that the company plans to raise their capex spending to $85 billion from the previous guidance of $75 billion. Management cited strong demand for the company’s cloud products and services as the primary reason behind raising capex spend.

More specifically, CFO Anat Ashkenazi cited “additional investment in servers, the timing of delivery of servers, and an acceleration in the pact of data centre construction,” as the factors behind the $10 billion jump in capex spend. Management also announced that higher depreciation, on account of the significant increase in Capex would act as a headwind to P&L. Finally, with respect to headcount, management announced that they would see a sequential increase in total headcount in the third quarter.

Search is Not Dead…At Least Not Yet

There have been a lot of claims in recent times that, with the advent of AI models such as ChatGPT and Perplexity, it would be curtains down for GOOGL’s search business. Q2 proved that these claims may have been exaggerated.

The company’s AI overviews have certainly proved to be popular and are driving higher search usage. Management announced that AI overviews has now over 2 billion monthly users across more than 200 countries, and the feature is available in 40 languages. Management also reported that AI overviews drove a 10% increase in search usage in Q2, which demonstrates that the company is leveraging AI to maintain its search dominance despite facing one of the most challenging competitive landscapes in recent memory. Moreover, the company’s newly launched AI mode already has over 100 million monthly active users despite being launched only in the U.S. and India.

All of these developments ensured that the search business not only went back to double-digit growth on a y/y basis but also comprehensively reversed its sequential decline that it witnessed last quarter.

One good quarter doesn’t make a trend, just like how one bad quarter doesn’t make one, either. However, the progress made by AI Overviews certainly suggests that the growth potential of the Search Business continues to remain strong - albeit it is not as bulletproof as it was in the past. Moreover, it is also encouraging to see how GOOGL’s AI features are ensuring that the Search segment is maintaining its popularity amongst younger users. For instance, the combination of lens or Circle to Search along with AI overviews saw strong traction among younger users.

The traditional way of searching via Google may no longer be a trend in the age of AI. However, the fact that GOOGL was able to transform its search business using its AI features certainly bodes well for the company’s future and certainly suggests that the demise of the company’s Search business may indeed have been exaggerated.

YouTube is Not Just Clocking Milestones

In my last article on GOOGL, I mentioned how in the previous quarter YouTube reached a historic milestone when it celebrated its 20th birthday. At the time, data from Nielsen had shown that the platform accounted for 12% of the total TV watch-time, which was a new record.

This quarter was no different, as the latest Nielsen data showed that consumers watch YouTube on TV more than on their phones or any other device, with an average of more than 1 billion hours each day. This viewing time is more than the total viewing time on Disney’s broadcast Network, on a dozen-plus cable channels, and on three streaming services - combined.

While milestones are great, it means nothing if they are not backed by revenue growth, as far as investors are concerned. In Q1, YouTube ads revenues barely crossed double-digit growth on a y/y basis. Moreover, they marked a double-digit decline sequentially, which was a concern. Q2, however, was different, with the segment registering a stronger growth on a y/y basis, and the sequential growth surging into positive territory.

Furthermore, management also announced that YouTube Shorts now average over 200 billion daily views and the company’s AI tools have improved not only recommendations but also offer features such as auto dubbing, which have increased engagement.

Finally, in addition to ads, the company is also focusing on growing the YouTube subscription businesses, as evidenced by the expansion of its premium light offerings to 15 new countries. Focusing on growing its subscription revenues is certainly the right step in my opinion, especially since in addition to content from independent creators, the platform also plays hosts to NFL Sunday Ticket, which costs the company $2 billion annually. Furthermore, companies like WGD and Paramount also offer consumers access to their content inside the YouTube app. As such, YouTube is well-positioned to diversify its revenue stream and should certainly capitalize on it.

Google Cloud Stands Out on Its Own, Justifying the Massive Capex Spend

Q2 saw Google Cloud generating strong revenues and margins, justifying the massive capex spend that GOOGL has been undertaking recently. More specifically, after generating a revenue growth of 28.1% y/y in Q1, the segment’s revenue growth in Q2 came in at 31.7%. Furthermore, if one looks at the performance sequentially, then Q2 was by far the best quarter ever, as the sequential growth came in at 11.1% compared to 2.6% in Q1. The last time it clocked double-digit sequential growth was in the fourth quarter of FY21, so it’s safe to say that the company’s capex spend is certainly paying off.

There was a lot of skepticism amongst analysts during the Q2 earnings call when management announced that the company would be increasing its capex spend by $10 billion to $85 billion for FY25. Management also mentioned that FY26 capex spend would also be higher, which further led analysts to question the timing of ROI. I have no issues with the company’s capex spending whatsoever. This is because not only is the spending translating into stronger growth for Google Cloud but it is also helping the company to revamp its Search business, as mentioned earlier.

There was a dip in growth in Google Cloud in Q1, which was concerning. But Q2 proved that it was merely a blip rather than a trend.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$256.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

22.4x

Projected EPS in the next twelve months

$12.32

Present Value of Projected EPS

$11.43

Sources: Alpha Vantage and Author’s Calculations.

I deployed an Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model using Machine Learning to forecast the GOOGL’s EPS in the next fourth quarters. The ARIMA model is beneficial for investors for several reasons such as more user-friendly and interpretable coefficients, automatic removal of mean-shifting trends, and the ability of the model to balance both fit and complexity through information criteria such as Akaike Information Criterion (AIC).

GOOGL’s EPS data was collected from Alpha Vantage for every fiscal quarter since 2005. In addition to EPS data, items from GOOGL’s income statement, balance sheet, and cash-flow statement were also collected. After data cleaning and merging datasets, the final sample consisted of data from 81 quarters.

The model was trained using 78 quarters with the remaining three quarters used for out-of-sample testing. The out-of-sample testing resulted in the following model metrics:

Metric

Value

Notes

Mean Absolute Error (MAE)

0.430

Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

0.430

RMSE = MAE implies that model errors are without large outliers

Mean Absolute Percentage Error

18.61%

10-20% range implies good forecasting

Given that the metrics suggest that the model is good for forecasting, the model was then used to project the EPS for the next four quarters. This was done by re-fitting the model on the entire 81-quarter series and then projecting four steps ahead (2025 Q3 – 2026 Q2). The projected EPS from the ARIMA model are as follows:

Quarter End

Projected EPS

30-09-2025

$2.96

31-12-2025

$3.00

31-03-2026

$3.14

30-06-2026

$3.22

Based on these projections, total EPS over the next twelve months would come in at $12.32, which would represent a y/y growth of 31.2%, which is in line with the company’s long-term average.

According to Alpha Spread, GOOGL’s cost of equity is 7.82%. Using this as the discount rate results in the present value of EPS as $11.43.

According to Seeking Alpha, GOOGL current trades at a forward P/E of 19.8x, significantly lower than its 5-year average of 24.6x. The ARIMA projections have limitations such as ignoring exogenous shocks, or failing to account for the evolution of earnings processes, there is the risk of overestimating the EPS projections. After accounting for this risk, I have assumed a market multiple of 22.4x for GOOGL for my projections.

A forward P/E of 22.4x and a projected EPS of $11.43 results in a price target of $256, which represents an upside of about 35% from current levels. Despite the significant upside, investors have to factor in that GOOGL’s momentum is weak, as evidenced by the short-term momentum grade of B- and an overall grade of C+ on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum Scale. As such, while I am maintaining my Buy rating on the name, a cost-averaging strategy is recommended here.

Risk Factors

The main risk factor to my bull case remains unchanged, which is the regulatory overhang that continues to be a problem. Next month, the company is likely to hear a judgment related to the trial that challenges Google's monopoly in search and advertising. The company could also lose it’s exclusivity rights with Apple (AAPL) as a result of this trial, which could significantly impact its top- and bottom-lines.

The other risk is the potential timeline with respect to its return on its capex investments. While the company has significantly made progress with respect to its AI features, the significant increase in capex spending that is expected to continue well into 2026 does lead to the question as to when the inflection point would be achieve with respect to growth. This is yet another factor that long-term investors have to consider.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another strong quarter from GOOGL, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-lines. The search business returned to double-digit growth, and YouTube saw continued momentum. The biggest standout to me was unsurprisingly Google Cloud, whose revenue growth and margin expansion were strong enough to justify the company’s massive capex spending. Moreover, its AI investments are starting to pay off and against all odds, the company’s Search Business continues to remain relevant even in the age of AI.

From a valuation perspective, my ARIMA model projects strong EPS growth in the next twelve months, which makes the company very attractive at current levels. However, given the relative weak momentum, while I am maintaining my Buy rating on the stock, I do recommend a cost-averaging strategy to get in the name.

Q2 was an impressive quarter for GOOGL. But if investors must take home one key message from the quarter, it is that Search continues to be sexy.
